title: Visible Value: Wage Determination in MLS
callout: MLS doesn't pay defenders. Across every position, better defensive output is associated with lower wages. Attack and progression are rewarded. Defense is invisible to the market.
description: 440 outfield players, 2024 MLS season. FBref performance data, MLSPA salaries, Google Trends popularity. Fixed-effects panel regressions with clustered standard errors.

items.caption: Salary by roster designation
items.finding: Label precedes labor. Designated Players earn ~4× a regular roster player before a single action is counted.

items.caption: Performance components vs salary
items.finding: Four components slope up. One slopes down.

items.alt: Defense Score vs Log Salary by Position
items.caption: Defense score vs salary — by position
items.finding: Five positions. Five negative slopes. No exceptions. Defend more, earn less(?!)

items.caption: Overall performance vs salary
items.finding: Messi top right, Afrifa bottom right. Busquets top left. The gap between the line and where players sit is the market's mispricing.

items.alt: Popularity vs Log Salary by Roster Designation
items.caption: Popularity vs salary — by designation
items.finding: Fame pays — but only for Designated Players. For everyone else, more Google searches barely moves the needle.

items.alt: Marginal Effect of Popularity across Performance
items.caption: Marginal effect of fame across performance levels
items.finding: Below-average performer? Being popular hurts your wage. Above-average? Fame amplifies it. The market pays for celebrity on top of output — not instead of it.

items.alt: Club Fixed Effects relative to League Average
items.caption: Club wage premium / discount — relative to league average
items.finding: Red Bulls and Inter Miami pay the most above market. Houston and Charlotte the most below. Controlling for who they signed — this is front-office philosophy, made visible.
